original_hypothesis.title: NextGenAdmins allows any global admin to grant themselves function/collection admin rights (privilege escalation within intended role hierarchy)
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenAdmins.AdminRequired allows either a global admin (adminPermissions[msg.sender]==true) OR the owner to call registerFunctionAdmin/registerBatchFunctionAdmin/registerCollectionAdmin. This means global admins can arbitrarily grant themselves (or others) any function-level and collection-level rights, bypassing the documented invariant that only global admins registered by the owner can register function/collection admins (and potentially contradicting separation of duties expectations).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: If the intended governance model assumes global admins cannot mint unlimited tokens / change critical wiring unless explicitly granted per-function, this code makes that assumption false: being a global admin effectively allows self-assignment to any privileged selector across the system.
deployment_assumptions: At least one non-owner address is set as global admin in production
Protocol intends function/collection admins to be more limited than global admins
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Docs state 'Function and Collection admins can only be registered by Global Admins' (not owner-only), but if global admin set is broader than intended, they can escalate. No further restrictions exist in code.

proof_of_signal.pattern: AdminRequired modifier: (adminPermissions[msg.sender]==true) || (_msgSender()==owner()); used to guard admin-granting functions

preconditions: Attacker controls a global admin address (adminPermissions[attacker]==true)
System relies on function/collection admin segmentation for safety
Attacker can compute/select target selectors for privileged operations

initial_code_evidence.suspicious_location: NextGenAdmins.sol AdminRequired is used for granting lower-tier rights
initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: role that is supposed to be high-level can mint itself arbitrary finer-grained privileges
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Global admin becomes universal admin across system by self-registering all selectors
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate 'grantor' role; no allowlist of selectors; no owner-only constraint for certain selectors

analysis_questions: Is global admin intended to already be omnipotent (then not a bug), or is it intended to be limited to granting others scoped permissions?
Which privileged functions across Minter/Core/Randomizers rely on functionAdmin checks and would become callable by attacker after self-grant?
Are there monitoring/operational controls expected off-chain instead?
validation_requirements: Enumerate selectors of privileged functions and simulate a global admin self-granting them
Verify that the granted permissions bypass any additional on-contract checks

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If attacker obtains or is assigned global admin, they call registerBatchFunctionAdmin(attacker, [selectors...], true) to grant themselves every sensitive function, then update core/minter/randomizer wiring or drain funds via emergencyWithdraw.
prerequisites: Global admin compromise or over-broad assignment (off-chain governance issue)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: High: enables calling emergencyWithdraw, wiring updates, metadata mutation, etc., depending on selector list
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational (revoke admin); on-chain damage may be irreversible

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting intended role hierarchy constraints

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: On-chain admin registry events/transactions for selector grants

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
